item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The comment section here basically birthed the horizon initiative. Neat!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image could not be properly sourced, and is thus assumed to be non compliant. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this scip works for me due to the underlying sense of sadness and loss combined with the total lack of world ending horror. It also introduced me to a new GOI to read the tales of when I get a chance so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t understand why Bélibaste refers to the (hypothetical) possibility of him being unbound. As perfectus, he has already undergone the consolamentum, and iirc no-one was allowed to receive it more than once: if you sinned afterwards, too bad, better luck next lifetime.

Other than that minor quibble, I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice Scip, it is very apparent that a lot of research went into writing it, and the discussion page for it lead to the creation of one of the most influential GoIs.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm… evil flesh god. Possible Sarkic ties?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this kind of religious-y scips. And I agree fully with the interviewer in the last addendum: There is joy to be found in life, even without any hope of an afterlife. Many times people have asked me if it isn't kind of sad to be an agnostic and I have answered just that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I consider this a work of beauty, and worthy of much more than a +1, but thats what I have to offer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now THAT would be interesting - like, a movement to try recapture the ability to transcend, using SCPs somehow. A GoI that's not directly antagonistic for a change. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Need to read it again, I think, but worth upvoting just for the color and imagination— I love anything using a religious heresy that is written in a way that doesn't make me want to punch me head through a wall. Not sure if the conversation at the end is just meh or detracts from the article… but on the whole this was an interesting read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather like this. The context given by the Horizon Initiative (about whom I am still very much on the fence) tale helps this a great deal. The "Historical Background" seemed a bit redundant, but that's mostly because I studied the Cathars a little bit in undergrad. The interview log was very nice and wistful, although "meat marionette" was a bit strange.

Plus to the 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To those interested in the Horizon Initiative, it's first tale is up: Fire on the Horizon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This wouldn't be the first time someone's confusion regarding an eagle and a melting rainbow man has caused trouble for me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea. A religious GoI very relatable to, not just straight fiction like CotBG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw the idea being chucked around on IRC and wasn't sure it could be done convincingly. This, however, is excellent - I echo Magnarius' comments that it's great to see something thought-provoking that isn't a doomsday scenario; the opposite, in fact. I wonder like Wishun whether agents inside the Foundation had the last initiate killed to preserve the material plane - if they did come into possession of a copy of the rite, presumably they would keep it in a safe and classify it as an existential threat, as protecting the world from the preternatural necessarily means forcing people to live in the mundane.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy the depth of history in this article. I've read a bit about the Albigensian heresy, but never sought out more information, and I might now. I love any SCP that promotes that, of course.

The subtle sadness and loss of the article is excellently portrayed, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm looking forward to reading that.

I also see a lot of great story potential in The Horizon Initiative. I like the fundamental conflict between a faith-driven containment effort and the Foundation's much more secular, rationalist, science-based approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Off-topic, but I keep mixing your posts up for mine because the thumbnail of your wikidot avatar looks like the thumbnail for that eagle I had a while back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I now have an idea for a first tale. A cardinal arrives to the Armenian Quarter of old Jerusalem, and enters what seems to be an abandoned building. In there are three chairs, shrouded in darkness. He reports his success at using the Foundation to destroy the last remnant of the Montsegur Loyalists without it realizing it. After that, he asks for permission to begin action against a more serious threat. After arguments relating to all sorts of historical events, the middle chair, a spokesman for a subgroup Beni Shamai, approves, as does the right chair, the spokesman for Atibba al-Kitab. Finally, the left chair, of the Occulti Luminis, relents.

They then go on to complain about how the eastern wing of their organization are making trouble.

E: I also want to make them a fairly recent organization, only a few decades old. Thus, they still have all this strife between the different sects and a whole lot of internal bickering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea. Go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Naturally. I just like the idea of at least some branches of the major religions, some of which are so openly antagonistic to each other, secretly working together for some common goal, maybe suppressing dangerous new religions or trying to destroy the memory of old dangerous religions. They could have some interesting connections to groups like the Fifth and CotBG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that could be pretty interesting as long as it's incorporated well. I get the feeling something like that could feel forced and silly if not used right. I'd really like to see what could be done with that, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I'm kinda considering making a religious GoI on that. Not just the Vatican, though, but a union of all major religions. They have meeting at various sacred sites around the globe and such, maybe call themselves something like "The Horizon Initiative". Could be neat. Hell, if I had to make a Cathar GoI, I'll call them something like the Montsegur Loyalists, after the last Cathar stronghold to fall in the crusade. I think more religious GoI's have potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always kind of secretly wanted to see a GOC style Group of Interest run by the Vatican.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like it in chat, like it now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can almost see modern day Cathars being a GOI themselves, trying to find ways to revive the faith in the modern day using anomalous objects. It'd be a nice deviation from groups such as the Church of the Broken God or the Fifthists whose SCPs tend to be mild to serious threats to humans/humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an interesting idea for a tale. Maybe something even more out there, like a union of modern religions against anything disturbing the status que- that is, members from various religions trying to wipe out memory of old religions and exterminate new ones, and any SCP concerning them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Catharism seems so perfectly tailored for an scp that I am still a little shocked that it was a real faith.

I can't help but wonder if that steering malfunction was random chance, or if there is something larger going on behind the scenes. That sort of unlikely but possible explanation feels a lot like the mob informant who "hangs himself" in his prison cell without anyone noticing before he can testify.

There might be a good tale there, or even a seed for a GoI. The modern Inquisition, dedicated to eliminating the "blasphemies that mock God's creation" and "defending the faith of the flock" by "shielding them from the devil's tricks". A bit more insidious and subtler than the GOC, with infiltrators most everywhere. They generally don't interfere with Foundation operations, because containment and protecting normalcy has a lot of overlap with their own goals. But they do arrange little accidents for people/things like this that are more directly in conflict with their dogma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Historical SCP and with a hell of a unlucky future. I still wonder what happened to the last guy, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You published it :D
It's good :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting article. I wonder whether the god of flesh and the god of spirit are real, or whether this SCP just made those who touch it believe? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm….not sure how much of this I understand and like. It's certainly interesting enough to make it a no-vote for me, though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, and thanks again to all the people who helped shape this article in the chat and forums. It would be a much lesser piece without you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly the kind of article that I like to see.

It takes a very interesting period in history, doesn't turn it in to a doomsday scenario, and instead explores the actual issue.

I can see the effort and scholarship put in to this, and award you my upvote. Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still not certain exactly how this thing works, but I'd say that it's a decent article.

Good enough for an upvote at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My brain is a little befuddled at the moment, but I like it. Uncertain how much of me liking it comes from having Dmatix explain it to me, though. Comments? (on the clarity issue, I mean.)
